---
title: "Lab13"
author: "Vandana Brahmasa"
date: "2025-05-01"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
a. Coin toss
```{r}
sample(c(-10,10), size=10, prob=c(0.5, 0.5), replace= TRUE)
```
b.  Now you can play the coin toss game against the house. If it comes up head, you earn $9, 800. Otherwise
you owe the house $10, 000. The %2 difference in payoff is called the house edge. Assume the coin is fair –
the chance of head or tail is 50/50. If you play the coin toss once, what is the casino’s expected profit? What is the probability that the casino makes money? You don’t need simulation to answer this question.
```{r}
#payoff to casino: -9800 for H and 10000 for T
casino_h <- -9800
casino_t <- 10000
exp_casino_payoff <- (0.5* casino_h) + (0.5* casino_t)
exp_casino_payoff
```
c. Now let’s change the stake of the coin tossing game. If it comes up head, you earn $98. Otherwise you
owe the house $100. The house edge is still 2%. If you play the coin toss 100 times (so that the total stake is
comparable to part b), what is the casino’s expected profit? What is the probability that the casino makes
money? Use simulation to answer this question.
```{r}
#simulate 100 coin toss games
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

# Number of games
n_games <- 100

# Simulate the outcomes: 1 = Head, 0 = Tail
tosses <- sample(c(0, 1), size = n_games, prob = c(0.5, 0.5), replace = TRUE)

# Calculate casino profit per toss:
# Head (1): casino loses $98 → -98
# Tail (0): casino gains $100 → +100
casino_profits <- ifelse(tosses == 1, -98, 100)

# Total profit over 100 games
total_profit <- sum(casino_profits)
print(paste("Total profit for the casino:", total_profit))

#simulate this experiment over 10000 iterations
# Run simulation over 10,000 repetitions
set.seed(123)
n_simulations <- 10000
profits <- numeric(n_simulations)

for (i in 1:n_simulations) {
  tosses <- sample(c(0, 1), size = 100, prob = c(0.5, 0.5), replace = TRUE)
  profit <- sum(ifelse(tosses == 1, -98, 100))
  profits[i] <- profit
}

# Expected profit (average)
mean_profit <- mean(profits)
print(paste("Expected profit for the casino over 100 games:", round(mean_profit, 2)))

# Probability casino makes money
prob_profitable <- mean(profits > 0)
print(paste("Probability that the casino makes money:", round(prob_profitable, 3)))

```
d. Let’s lower the stake of the coin tossing game even futher. If it comes up head, you earn $0.98. Otherwise
you owe the house $1. The house edge is still 2%. If you play the coin toss 10,000 times, what is the casino’s
expected profit? What is the probability that the casino makes money? Use simulation to answer this
question.

```{r}
# simulation over 10000 games
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

# Number of games
n_games <- 10000

# Simulate coin tosses: 1 = head, 0 = tail
tosses <- sample(c(0, 1), size = n_games, prob = c(0.5, 0.5), replace = TRUE)

# Compute casino profits: head = -0.98, tail = +1
profits <- ifelse(tosses == 1, -0.98, 1)

# Total profit
total_profit <- sum(profits)
print(paste("Total casino profit from 10,000 games:", round(total_profit, 2)))

# simulate 10000 game over 10000 iterations
# Run 10,000 simulations of 10,000 coin tosses each
set.seed(123)
n_simulations <- 10000
profits_total <- numeric(n_simulations)

for (i in 1:n_simulations) {
  tosses <- sample(c(0, 1), size = 10000, prob = c(0.5, 0.5), replace = TRUE)
  profits <- ifelse(tosses == 1, -0.98, 1)
  profits_total[i] <- sum(profits)
}

# Expected profit
mean_profit <- mean(profits_total)
print(paste("Expected profit:", round(mean_profit, 2)))

# Probability that the casino makes money
prob_profit <- mean(profits_total > 0)
print(paste("Probability casino makes money:", round(prob_profit, 3)))

```
